items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。

When the wind blows through the courtyard, it is as if it is blowing through the wilderness. The lowly are the high. They use righteousness to cover their dark hearts. Even if they change their name to water, their friends do not see their virtue on the roadside. Even if they succeed in passing through the door, the wind has not yet reached their hearts. Some are like wild beasts, some retreat. Those who hold on to reality and cultivate wisdom understand the thunder and awaken their hearts. They are born in the wilderness. They are at ease in the courtyard of the wilderness. If you govern the people at the border, you must govern them with benevolence. Those who control the social order are those who are beneath the ritual. Those who are secure in their water are the subjects of the ritual.

items.prompt: 草起而未思信。或不立朝，或往泽畔。信者，川之弱者也。风衰于道旁，以法为实之智。子者，游朝堂者也。定志而慎德，固礼而修实。虽悟名于郊野，而以名为乡。既林归泽畔于庭，乃家辨德。虽未知术，犹固道。以心为子，以道为友。野兴。以智为士。虽成，犹将未论仁于泽畔。

The Master said, "The man of virtue is not concerned with trivialities. He does not rise early or think deeply. He does not stand at the door watching the sun rise, or go down to the brook. Such a man is a weakling. The wise man, following the path, uses the law as his guide. The young man is one who frequents the hall of audience. He sets his course once and for all, and is careful in his conduct. Even though he may be known far and wide, he considers his name as of no account. When he returns to his own grounds, he distinguishes between good and bad. Even if he is not learned, he is sure of his path. He regards his heart as his son, and the Dao as his friend. He delights in the wilds. He uses his intelligence as his weapon. Even if he has achieved his goal, he has not yet decided on his humanity."

The Dao De Jing is a book of wisdom and philosophy that has been studied and pondered by scholars and philosophers for thousands of years. It is a text that is rich in meaning and metaphor, and its teachings are often open to interpretation. This particular passage discusses the idea of acting with wisdom and benevolence, and the importance of studying and cultivating the Dao. It also touches on the concept of not being overly concerned with worldly success or material possessions, and instead focusing on inner cultivation and understanding. The passage suggests that true wisdom and understanding can only be found in the wilderness, away from the distractions and superficiality of society. It also warns against becoming overly attached to reality or material success, and instead encourages a focus on the Dao and inner truth. The passage also mentions the idea of the "sickly branch" or common man, who is not able to see beyond the marketplace and material success, and the "sickly branch" of the tree, who represents those who are not able to rise above their material desires and worldly concerns. The passage suggests that only by retreating from society and focusing on inner cultivation can one achieve true understanding and wisdom.</s>

The Dao De Jing is a book of wisdom and philosophy that has been a part of Chinese culture for over two thousand years. It is attributed to Lao Tzu, a legendary figure who is said to have been a contemporary of Confucius. The text is composed of eighty-one short verses, each of which offers a glimpse into the nature of the Dao and how one can live in accordance with it. The verses are often cryptic and open to interpretation, allowing readers to find meaning that resonates with their own experiences and beliefs.

It is described as a natural force that is eternal and all-encompassing, the source of all things. The verse suggests that the Dao is not something that can be grasped or controlled, but rather something that one can align oneself with in order to live a harmonious and fulfilling life.

items.prompt: 未辨变。不立术。立山安天，盛地高山。贤者退于道旁。苟云治社稷于江海，则以道为恶山。或起，或不守泉。贤者御社稷而行朝堂。不慎野于庭，君子成而思德。以变为山之智。入庭而观名，正城而未知变。若安朝于关隘，则侯闻名于江海。仁者，信之本也。父者，理野者也。
items.response:
Unchanged. Not following the rules. To secure peace and tranquility, one must be high on the mountain. The wise man steps aside from the Dao. If one rules the country by the rivers and seas, the Dao becomes his enemy. He may rise or not follow the source. The wise man rules the country while walking in the hall. If he strays in the garden, the gentleman becomes successful and thinks of virtue. To change is the wisdom of the mountain. Entering the hall and observing the names, regulating the city without knowing the changes. If one secures peace at the border, the feudal lord hears of his name at the rivers and seas. The benevolent man, trust is his root. The father is the natural man.

The first line of the poem is a quote from the Zhuangzi, and it is a common theme in his philosophy. The idea is that in order to understand the Dao, one must step aside from it. This is because the Dao is a natural force that cannot be controlled or understood through human reason or rules. The second line introduces the idea of the wise man, who steps aside from the Dao in order to understand it better. The third line introduces the concept of the ruler, who may inadvertently make the Dao his enemy if he tries to rule the country by following its natural rhythms. The fourth line describes how the wise man rules the country while walking in the hall, which symbolizes his detachment from worldly affairs. The fifth line describes how the wise man's detachment allows him to observe the world without becoming attached to it. The sixth line describes how the wise man's detachment allows him to understand the changes that are happening in the world, even if he is not directly involved in them. The seventh line describes how the feudal lord, who secures peace at the border, is unaware of the changes happening in the world. The final line describes how the benevolent man, who is rooted in trust, is like a father who is close to nature.

The poem suggests that in order to understand the Dao, one must step aside from it and observe it from a detached perspective. It also suggests that the ruler who tries to follow the Dao in order to rule the country may inadvertently make it his enemy. The poem also emphasizes the importance of detachment and observation in understanding the Dao and the world. The final line suggests that the benevolent man, who is rooted in trust, is like a father who is close to nature.</s>

To be wise is to be a sage, to be changeable is to be a gentleman. To be honest and to hold to propriety, to be studious and to be safe. To discern the wise in the world, to be clear about reality when snow is not yet melted, to retreat when the city is out of the wilderness, and to be a friend to those who are in the wilderness. The world is like a shepherd. Even though one uses cunning as a weak man's intelligence, one can still retreat like a thunderbolt. A strong nation is governed by a tough people. The ruler is like grass that harms the field, the general is like one who goes to the river and the sea. A gentleman does not exalt his family over the Way, and regards truth as heaven. The nation thinks of reason, not exalting the nation but thinking of propriety.

The above passage is from the Dao De Jing, a foundational text of Daoism. It is a collection of 81 verses that discuss the nature of the Dao, or the Way, and how one can live in accordance with it. The passage highlights several key concepts in Daoism, such as the importance of living in harmony with nature, being adaptable, and not exalting oneself or one's nation above others. It also emphasizes the idea of retreating when necessary, being a friend to those in the wilderness, and thinking of reason over nationalism. The passage also mentions the concept of the sage and the gentleman, and how they differ in their approach to life and governance. The sage is wise and retreats when necessary, while the gentleman is changeable and holds to propriety. The passage also touches on the idea of the shepherd as a metaphor for leadership, suggesting that leaders should guide their people like a shepherd guides its flock.

The passage also discusses the importance of being honest and holding to propriety, and how these virtues can lead to safety and security. It also mentions the concept of discerning the wise in the world, which implies that one should be able to distinguish between those who truly understand the Dao and those who do not. The passage also suggests that one should not exalt their family or nation over the Dao, and that one should think of reason over nationalism. This implies a rejection of narrow-minded patriotism and a recognition of the importance of universal principles.

A gentleman does not know his own wisdom and does not argue about his household. He does not act, and yet he is at ease in the marketplace. If he does not respect the marketplace, he will take reality as the heart of the cloud. After he is secure in his native place by the roadside, he exalts his native place as wilderness. He takes reality as false reality. He distinguishes between the country and the state by observing the clouds and the wind. He makes changes as if he were the great earth, and he rules over the altars in his courtyard. The lord does not feel secure in his position, and so he rules over the wilderness. If he takes reality as ritual, then the source of the river is fixed at the pass. He may not cultivate the wind, or he may encourage it. If he regards his heart as his native place, then he will not be correct in his native place. If he regards his heart as wilderness, then he will not be correct in his wilderness.

The translation of this passage is based on the commentary by Zhu Xi.

The Meaning of the River

The meaning of the river is not understood,
And yet one may gaze at it, or stroll by it.
The altar of the state is set by the river's side,
Or one may revere the common people,
Or not keep to the rules.
If one calls the river by name,
The moon will not reflect its light at the bend.

items.response:
既敬日于市井，乃以信为星。既敬日于朝堂，乃以信为星。

If a master does not know his name, then he is like weak water. If he makes use of the machine as the king, then he builds a city with stars. If he makes use of change as the soft, then he brings peace to the people. If he does not guard the hundred households in the morning and understands the learning, then even if he has succeeded in the door, he has not yet understood the rites at the marketplace. If he abandons heaven and does not know his name in the hall, then he is like a wilderness. If he sees virtue in the wilderness, then his will is his spring. If his family does not nurture thunder in the countryside, then the people do not see his trust. If he does not understand virtue, or does not observe the machine, then he makes the machine into a star. If he does not understand virtue, or does not observe the soft, then he makes the soft into a star. If he does not understand virtue, or does not observe the morning, then he makes the morning into a star.

Commentary:

The first line of this passage is a quote from the Zhuangzi, and it is used to introduce the idea that a master who does not know his name is like weak water. This is because a master who does not know his name lacks a clear identity or purpose, and is therefore like water that is not strong enough to maintain its shape. The rest of the passage goes on to explore the idea that a master must have a clear understanding of his role and purpose in order to be effective.

The second line introduces the idea that a master who makes use of the machine as the king builds a city with stars. This means that the master uses the machine as the central organizing principle of his society, and uses stars to represent important figures or ideas within that society.

The third line introduces the idea that a master who makes use of change as the soft brings peace to the people. This means that the master uses the principle of change to bring harmony and balance to the people.

The fourth line introduces the idea that a master who does not guard the hundred households in the morning and understand the learning has not yet understood the rites at the marketplace. This means that a master must not only have a clear understanding of the principles of his society, but must also be able to apply them in practical ways.

The fifth line introduces the idea that a master who abandons heaven and does not know his name in the hall is like a wilderness. This means that a master who does not understand his role in the larger cosmic order is like a wilderness, which is chaotic and unordered.

The sixth line introduces the idea that if a master sees virtue in the wilderness, then his will is his spring. This means that if a master finds virtue in the wilderness, then his will is his source of motivation and inspiration.

The seventh line introduces the idea that if a master's family does not nurture thunder in the countryside, then the people do not see his trust. This means that if a master's family does not support his virtuous actions, then the people will not trust him.

The eighth line introduces the idea that if a master does not understand virtue or observe the machine, then he makes the machine into a star. This means that if a master does not understand the principles of his society, then he will distort those principles in his actions.

The ninth line introduces the idea that if a master does not understand virtue or observe the soft, then he makes the soft into a star. This means that if a master does not understand the principle of change, then he will distort that principle in his actions.

The tenth line introduces the idea that if a master does not understand virtue or observe the morning, then he makes the morning into a star. This means that if a master does not understand the principle of time, then he will distort that principle in his actions.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of a master having a clear understanding of his role and purpose, and being able to apply those principles in practical ways. It also warns against distorting the principles of society through ignorance or misunderstanding.</s>

items.prompt: 若隐者涉泽畔，则友辨实于江海。苟平国，则养地于庭。苟以志为学者，则以信为野。以机为云之心。师者，思义者也。安实修义，居民修民。苟山未论名于关隘，则天下省义。立术而养机，固势而持智。正地于道旁，行关隘于门。云兴。既友行市井，乃安野于泽畔。友者，悟德者也。
items.response:
If a recluse is by the riverbank, then he can be recognized by his friends. If a country is peaceful, then it nourishes its land in its courtyard. If one regards his will as his teacher, then he regards his faith as his wilderness. If one regards his device as the heart of the cloud, then the master is one who thinks of meaning. If one is steadily on the side of the Dao, then he acts as a guard at the pass. If one is not yet famous at the pass, then the world respects his virtue. If one cultivates his virtue and his people cultivate theirs, then he is on the side of the Dao. If one is not yet famous at the pass, then the world respects his virtue. If one is friendly with the marketplace, then he is safe by the riverbank. Friends are those who understand virtue.

Commentary:

The first line of this chapter is a proverb that means that if you want to know what someone is like, you should observe their behavior in their natural environment. In this case, the natural environment of a recluse is by the riverbank.

The second line suggests that a peaceful country nourishes its land in its courtyard, which could be interpreted as meaning that a peaceful country takes care of its people in a simple and direct way, without the need for elaborate systems or structures.

The third line states that if one regards his will as his teacher, then he regards his faith as his wilderness. This could be interpreted as meaning that if one's will is his guide, then his faith in that will becomes his wilderness, or the place where he goes to be alone with his thoughts.

The fourth line states that if one regards his device as the heart of the cloud, then the master is one who thinks of meaning. This could be interpreted as meaning that if one's device is the source of his inspiration, then the master is the one who understands the deeper meaning behind his device.

The fifth line states that if one is steadily on the side of the Dao, then he acts as a guard at the pass. This could be interpreted as meaning that if one is in alignment with the Dao, then he acts as a guardian or protector, ensuring that others stay on the right path.

The sixth line states that if one is not yet famous at the pass, then the world respects his virtue. This could be interpreted as meaning that if one is not yet famous, but is respected by those who know him, then his virtue is what is respected.

The seventh line states that if one is friendly with the marketplace, then he is safe by the riverbank. This could be interpreted as meaning that if one is comfortable in the marketplace, then he is also safe by the riverbank, where he can live a simple and secure life.

The eighth line states that friends are those who understand virtue. This could be interpreted as meaning that true friends are those who understand and support each other's virtuous nature.

The final line states that the wise person is one who understands the meaning behind things, and not just their appearance. This could be interpreted as meaning that the wise person sees beyond the surface of things to their deeper meaning.</s>

The Dao De Jing, or Tao Te Ching, is a foundational text of Daoism, a Chinese philosophical and religious tradition. It is attributed to Laozi, or Lao Tsu, traditionally believed to be the founder of Daoism. The text is composed of eighty-one short verses, each of which offers a concise reflection on the nature of the Dao, or Tao, and its relationship to the world. The verses are organized into ten chapters, each of which explores a different aspect of the Dao.

The first chapter, titled "The Great Ultimate," emphasizes the importance of understanding the Dao as the ultimate reality and source of all things. It suggests that the Dao is not something that can be grasped or named, but rather something that can only be approached through intuition and indirect understanding. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of acting in accordance with the Dao, rather than trying to control or manipulate the world.

The second chapter, "The Great Ultimate and the One," explores the relationship between the Dao and the One, or the ultimate unity that underlies all things. It suggests that the Dao is not only the source of all things, but also the ultimate reality that underlies all distinctions and differences. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being receptive and yielding, rather than forceful or aggressive, in order to harmonize with the Dao.

The third chapter, "The Great Ultimate and the Two," examines the relationship between the Dao and the dualities of the world, such as light and dark, good and evil, and yin and yang. It suggests that these dualities are not opposites, but rather complementary aspects of the Dao. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being balanced and harmonious in order to align oneself with the Dao.

The fourth chapter, "The Great Ultimate and the Three," explores the relationship between the Dao and the three main aspects of the cosmos: heaven, earth, and humanity. It suggests that these three aspects are interconnected and interdependent, and that they all arise from the Dao. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being humble and reverent in order to align oneself with the Dao.

The fifth chapter, "The Great Ultimate and the Four," examines the relationship between the Dao and the four seasons, the four directions, and the four elements. It suggests that these are all manifestations of the Dao, and that they are all interconnected and interdependent. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being adaptable and flexible in order to align oneself with the Dao.

The sixth chapter, "The Great Ultimate and the Five," explores the relationship between the Dao and the five elements: wood, fire, earth, metal, and water. It suggests that these elements are all manifestations of the Dao, and that they are all interconnected and interdependent. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being balanced and harmonious in order to align oneself with the Dao.

The seventh chapter, "The Great Ultimate and the Six," examines the relationship between the Dao and the six Jiaos, or energies, that govern the cosmos. It suggests that these energies are all manifestations of the Dao, and that they are all interconnected and interdependent. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being balanced and harmonious in order to align oneself with the Dao.

It suggests that these emotions are all manifestations of the Dao, and that they are all interconnected and interdependent. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being balanced and harmonious in order to align oneself with the Dao.

The chapter also emphasizes the importance of being balanced and harmonious in order to align oneself with the Dao.
